title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Joyce sits with her aging, twelve-year-old dog, referred to by various names including Tris, Tray, Tricky, Triffy, Trudie, Terry, Trixie, and Trix. Her landlady, Mrs. Barnes, brings Joyce a cup of hot tea and informs her that Barnes is unemployed again, creating financial uncertainty. Mrs. Barnes complains that the dog bit her earlier that morning while she was looking at Joyce's shoes, but Joyce defends the animal, explaining that it is protective of her belongings. After Mrs. Barnes leaves, Joyce expresses her deep anxiety to the dog regarding their situation.

Joyce reveals that she and the dog owe Mrs. Bunce four weeks' rent. She expresses frustration with the local men, noting that Mr. Bunce is often unemployed due to his drinking. Joyce reflects on her past, remembering happy times with the dog, her father, and Michael. She recalls Michael taking them to the countryside and how the dog supported her when she received a telegram regarding Michael's death. Joyce discloses that Michael gambled away his money, a fact she keeps secret from his aunts, who believe she is doing well.

Feeling exhausted and hungry at age twenty-nine, Joyce admits she feels much older and lacks the strength to beg for help, noting a failed attempt to visit her cousin, Charlotte Green, in Iling. She vows never to separate from the dog, despite its declining health and temperament. The passage ends when Joyce hears two knocks on the door downstairs.
